name: Vishing / Suspicious Phone Call Report
description: Internal form for employees to report suspicious phone calls or voicemails — callers claiming to be IT support, vendors, executives, banks, or government agencies, asking for credentials, MFA codes, payments, or remote-access permission. Severity escalates if the user provided anything to the caller.
title: Report a Suspicious Phone Call
intro: Got a phone call that felt off? Someone claiming to be IT, a vendor, an executive, your bank, or the government — asking you to share credentials, read an MFA code, install something, or send money? Report it here. Even if you didn't give them anything, the report helps us warn others and track active campaigns. You're not in trouble if you did engage with them — be honest so we can help.
submit_message: Thanks — your report is in the queue. If you shared anything with the caller, an analyst will reach out shortly. If you didn't, we'll still review and may follow up if we see a pattern.

fields.label: Your email
fields.help: So we can reach you if we need more details.

fields.label: Caller ID / phone number
fields.help: The number that showed up on your phone. Include country code if you saw it. 'Unknown' or 'Blocked' is also useful info.

fields.label: Who did the caller claim to be?
fields.help: The name and role they gave you, or the company they claimed to represent.
fields.sample_value: Adam from IT support — said he was on the internal help desk team

fields.label: How did they contact you?
options: Live phone call
Voicemail (no live conversation)
Video call (Zoom, Teams, etc.)
Robocall / automated voice
Callback after I called a number from somewhere else
Not sure

fields.label: What did they ask for?
options: My password or login credentials
An MFA / verification code from my phone or email
Remote access to my computer (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, Quick Assist)
Me to install or run something
Payment, wire transfer, or gift cards
Personal information (SSN, DOB, bank info)
Company information (org chart, customer list, project details)
Me to confirm or 'verify' details they already had
Other
sample_value: Remote access to my computer (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, Quick Assist)

fields.label: Did you give them anything?
fields.help: Be honest — this is the most important question on the form. You're not in trouble. Knowing the truth lets us protect you.
options: No, I gave them nothing and hung up
I confirmed some basic info (my name, that I work here, my role)
I read them an MFA / verification code
I gave them my password or credentials
I let them connect to my computer
I installed or ran something they asked me to
I sent money, gift cards, or payment info
Not sure
fields.sample_value: No, I gave them nothing and hung up

fields.label: What did they say? Tell us the story.
fields.help: Describe the call in your own words — pretext, urgency tactics, what felt off, what specific things they said.
fields.sample_value: Caller ID said it was our help desk number. Guy said 'Hi this is Adam from IT, we're seeing an outage with VPN connections from your laptop and need to push a fix.' He read off my full name, my manager's name, and what office I'm in. Then he asked me to confirm my password so he could 'verify access.' I said I'd call the help desk back directly and he got pushy, said the outage would spread if we didn't fix it now. I hung up and called the help desk on the internal number — they have no Adam and no outage.

fields.label: Anything else?
fields.help: Optional. Other coworkers contacted? Caller's accent or background noise? Anything memorable.
fields.sample_value: Two people in my Slack channel mentioned getting similar calls this week, both claiming to be from IT.

alert_template.title: Vishing attempt reported by {{reporter_email}}
